for those of you who don't know, my name is Nick Rowin, and I'm a senior leader and

former chair of Wellington City Council's Accessibility
Advisory Group. But I appear
in front of you today in my personal capacity.
I want to be clear, I support the principle
that disability support services deserve a
durable legislative foundation.
The absence of one has cost this country decades of
fragmentation and inequality.
The objective is sound, but this bill does not deliver it
in any way, shape, or form. I've identified five
structural failures serious enough to require substantial amendments
before this bill proceeds any further.
Clause 8
inverts self-determination by forcing
disabled people to exhaust their own resources, meager as they are,
and that of their families, before the Crown accepts any
obligation.
That is not person-centered.
That is beginning with the assumption that the person is
a burden.
Second,
this bill violates the New Zealand's obligations under the UN
Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities.
The government's own regulatory impact statement acknowledges that
consultation required under the convention did not
occur before the bill was introduced to the House.
The government introduced legislation affecting 55,000 disabled
New Zealanders while admitting it broke the rules.
And this select committee process, while it's nice to meet all the members
here today, does not make up for that breach.
Third, natural justice.
The Supreme Court unanimously held up the rights of
family
carers in the Fleming decision in December 2025.
This bill reverses that decision within months,
before a single alternative support was in place.
That is the definition of what natural justice
exists to prevent
Fourth, ministerial power.
This bill lets a single minister design, alter, and
potentially restrict disability support by written
notice
with no parliamentary oversight, no independent review,
and most
concerning, no floor of entitlement in primary legislation.
Good law should not work that way.
And lastly,
and fifth, families. 63% of
carers of disabled children say they just do not have enough money to
support their children or themselves.
This bill responds to this situation
by extending family financial obligations across
aunts, uncles, cousins, and grandparents with no
additional funding.
That is not a carer strategy. That is cost
shifting onto people who are already at breaking point.
I am here to ask this committee to do what the government
would not do.
Consult, amend, and put it right.
The test is simple: Does this legislation leave
disabled New Zealanders more secure or less secure?
With this bill as currently drafted, the answer is clear.
I ask you to use the power that you have as members of this select committee
to put things right.
Thank you. I'm happy to take questions.

Thanks, Nick.

Thank you for this opportunity to speak to you today, not as an advocate or a
natural support, but as a mother of two adult sons who require
full-time residential care. My name is Helena Faiti, although
in this context, I am proudly Adrian and Don's mum.
I'm here to share why families like mine are worried and concerned by the
Disability Support Services Bill, and why I believe it must be
rejected in its current form.
For years, parents like me have worked with schools, health providers,
therapists, MSD, and other government agencies and their
contracted providers to ensure our children are safe,
respected, and supported in New Zealand as equal citizens.
This bill, in its detail, threatens that foundation in three
ways.
Firstly, it abdicates the state's responsibility.
This bill states that families and whanau have
responsibilities for the well-being of their members in the first
instance. Nearly all the families I know operate this
family-first principle and have done so from day zero.
Mm.
Frankly, formalizing this principle is harmful.
It deflects the Crown's duty onto the shoulders of exhausted and aging
parents. My sons are now 32 and 38.
They need 24/7 care and supervision, and I no longer have the
energy to provide this safely in my home day in, day out.
By making state care sound like a contribution or a favor
rather than a right, this legislation is signaling a silent
withdrawal of government support
For most New Zealand parents, legal responsibility for a child ends
when that child turns 18. Our sons, as I said, are now 32
and 38, well beyond that age. Yet under this bill,
families like ours would still be considered
responsible in the first instance.
As the definition of family in this bill casts a very wide and
interesting net, it tells us that under the first principle,
public funded support is conditional upon utilizing available
family and community resources.
In families like ours, once my husband and I are no longer up to this
role, this would mean that the responsibility lies with our
brothers and sisters and uncles and aunts.
And if none of them are available, step-families, cousins, nieces,
nephews will have to take up this role. This feels unsafe.
Secondly, I am grateful that no one support changes for three
years. But in 2019, this bill will give
sweeping powers to the minister to set up new programs with
minimal public or any parliamentary scrutiny.
It leaves the door wide open for the possible future income and
asset testing without any baseline protections in law.
Families who have planned and worked to secure a safe living environment for
their loved ones are now living in fear that the rug will be pulled out from under
them, and that they will be forced to cover impossible to predict
costs out of their own pensions. Thirdly,
I cannot ignore the context in which this bill was rushed to Parliament.
It was drafted and directly in response to the Supreme Court ruling that
recognized some family carers as government employees.
Instead of using that landmark decision
to honor and fairly compensate families for the immense work they do,
this legislation was designed to shield the Crown from further litigation and
fiscal risk.
It prioritizes balancing the budget over the dignity and equality
of our disabled loved ones. This makes me sad and worried.
Love and necessity drive me and my husband to care for our sons,
but love should never be a substitute for government
infrastructure. The state has a moral obligation to provide
sustainable long-term support for New Zealand's most vulnerable
citizens. This bill is telling families like mine that
our equality in modern society is some sort of fiscal problem to be
managed and controlled.
Are we now somehow a burden or a second-class citizen?
Disabled New Zealanders and their whanau deserve the legislative framework that
protects their rights, not one that treats them as obstacles to
avoid. I urge you to reject this bill.
We must go back to the drawing board and build a system
with the disability community, not a system done to them.
Well done.

Thank you.

All right. Next speaker is Assistive Technology Suppliers New Zealand, Gary
Holmes, the CEO.
Welcome, Gary.
Thank you.
ATS-NZ represents the businesses that manufacture, import, and deliver
assistive technology across New Zealand.
Wheelchairs, communication devices, home modification, and
mobility aids. We are the supply chain.
We are not a disability advocacy group, and we want to be clear about that.
Our concern with this bill is commercial and practical, and they come from
our direct experience of what is happening when DSS funding
settings change without notice.
So our biggest concern is clause 11 (3) and .
These subclauses give a future minister explicit statutory authority to
introduce means testing for DSS eligibility by written
notice, without a parliamentary vote, and without any obligation to
consult the sector. DSS has never been means tested.
If the government wants to change that, it is a significant enough decision to
go through the parliamentary process.
The second concern sits alongside it in clause eight, and it's already been
referred to, the Families First principle.
Where a family's resources and natural supports are assessed
before equipment is prescribed, fewer people will receive
funded assisted technology, not because the clinical need has gone
away, but because the framework allows family circumstances to be
weighed first. Our members see family situations up close.
Financial capacity on paper does not always mean a disabled
family member's needs are prioritized in practice.
When DSS funding settings can change by written notice, suppliers
can't plan. We carry inventory, we employ specialist
technicians. We make workforce and stock decisions
12 months or even more ahead. A sudden eligibility
change gives us nothing to work with, and the practical result is reduced
investment, withdrawal from harder to reach markets,
particularly regional communities, and ultimately less competition,
which means higher procurement costs- For the funders this bill is
designed to serve. The government has its own interest in a healthy
supplier market. We're asking for four things, and none of them change the
framework. One, consult the supply sector before
ministerial programs are made with a minimum of 30-day notice
period written into the bill. Two, require a parliamentary vote to
introduce means testing. Remove or constrain Clause 11
(3) and .
Amend Clause 8 so that the families first principle cannot
override a clinical AT prescription from an
occupational therapist or a specialist assessor.
And more time on this process. As a couple of people have already referred to,
three weeks is really not enough.
Mm.
Just in closing, the government's own regulatory impact statement
acknowledges that there was no consultation before this bill was introduced.
The supply side of the disability support system was not at the
table. We're asking for the committee to recommend the safeguards that make
this framework workable for suppliers and, more
importantly, for the disabled New Zealanders who depend on the equipment that we
deliver. Thank you, Mr. Chairman.
Mm.

The next submitter is the Cerebral Palsy Society of New Zealand.
Daniel and Claire.

Hokai Nokorangi.

My name's Daniel Clay. I'm the chair of the Cerebral Palsy Society.
Cerebral palsy is the most common childhood physical disability and affects
approximately 9,000 New Zealanders.
Many people with cerebral palsy have high and complex needs and need significant
support.
The vast majority rely on disability support services rather than ACC.
The committee has asked submitters what their biggest concern is and what changes
they want to see. On changes, I've tracks and changes to the
infamous Clause 8-
Yeah
... and a few others, which I can hand around while I'm
continuing with my statement.
And I'll get to that at the end.
Our biggest concern with is that the bill assumes that families can provide more
support if government support is reduced.
The evidence shows otherwise. We surveyed 140 members
for our submission who rely on those services.
Almost half of the respondents reported providing more than 40
hours of unpaid care every week, and more than a
quarter provide over 80 hours of unpaid care
a week. Many whanau are effectively providing a second full-time
job in unpaid care. They're already filling significant gaps in the
current system. And just on the question about codifying the current system
or current practice, if that's the case, that's not right.
The current system is not working and whanau are filling the gaps,
and what this bill does is make it worse.
Most respondents, unsurprisingly, told us that the current disability support
services do not meet their disability needs. The consequences are substantial.
The vast majority of respondents reported impacts such as caregiver exhaustion,
burnout, mental health impacts, relationship strain, lost employment
opportunities, and financial pressure.
These are not hypothetical future risks of a CBA.
These are occurring now. More than two-thirds said they could not
provide more support if funding were reduced, and that providing
significantly more care themselves would simply be unmanageable.
Many respondents also expressed deep concern about the future.
Parents are already providing substantial support well into their children's
adulthood, and are worried about what will happen when they age,
when age, illness, or death means they can no longer
provide support. This highlights an important issue with the bill.
There's a significant difference between the responsibility of parents
raising a disabled child and the expectations placed on family and
whanau members, widely defined, of disabled adults.
Those situations should not be treated as equivalent.
Reducing support would also have wider serious economic consequences for
individuals and the economy. Most respondents told us that reduced
support would affect their household's ability to remain in paid employment.
And over half said that means testing would have a major negative impact, with
less than 10% anticipating no impact.
In other words, reducing support does not simply shift
responsibility from government to families.
It would also reduce workforce participation and increase financial
pressures, driving more families and whanau of disabled people into
economic hardship. We submitted that the bill should not proceed in its current
form. However, if the committee wishes to proceed, we provided proposed
amendments. These amendments, first and importantly, making clear that
the government has primary responsibility for disability-related support
needs, while families play an important complementary role.
Secondly, removing the provisions enabling means testing, and third, require
meaningful consultation on future support programs.
In summary, our message is simple.
Families are already doing an extraordinary amount, more than they should.
Many are at breaking point. They cannot do more.
The practical effect of the bill is therefore likely to be reduced support for
disabled people, not increased family care.
We ask the committee to recognize that supporting disabled people is not a matter
of charity or family capacity.
Mm.
It is a fundamental responsibility of the government.
Kia ora, and I welcome your questions.

of disability support,
